raw_response: {"rating": -1.9, "lean": "Lean Left", "explanation": "This is a news report. The author’s own framing and source choices lean left by validating pro-choice-aligned datasets as authoritative and emphasizing that abortions rose despite bans, without balancing perspectives or alternative datasets. These consistent editorial choices support a Lean Left rating. Detailed Analysis: (1) The author characterizes the study as “the most comprehensive account of all abortions by state since the overturning of Roe v. Wade,” an endorsement that elevates pro-choice-aligned datasets (Guttmacher, WeCount) without caveats or alternative sources (e.g., CDC/state data); (2) Section header and lead framing emphasize that “more women from states with bans obtained abortions” in 2023 than in 2020, highlighting the ineffectiveness of bans—a narrative commonly aligned with left-leaning critiques; (3) Explanatory prose centers on mechanisms circumventing bans (interstate travel, mail-order pills under shield laws) in neutral-to-validating terms, with no countervailing legal, enforcement, or policy perspectives; (4) Declines are downplayed (Texas decrease labeled “small,” Oklahoma decline discounted by noting an “unusually large” 2020 baseline), reinforcing the overarching narrative of increases; (5) Only one expert voice is featured and no opposing or balancing viewpoints are included, reflecting one-sided sourcing in a politically charged topic."}

parsed_output.explanation: This is a news report. The author’s own framing and source choices lean left by validating pro-choice-aligned datasets as authoritative and emphasizing that abortions rose despite bans, without balancing perspectives or alternative datasets. These consistent editorial choices support a Lean Left rating. Detailed Analysis: (1) The author characterizes the study as “the most comprehensive account of all abortions by state since the overturning of Roe v. Wade,” an endorsement that elevates pro-choice-aligned datasets (Guttmacher, WeCount) without caveats or alternative sources (e.g., CDC/state data); (2) Section header and lead framing emphasize that “more women from states with bans obtained abortions” in 2023 than in 2020, highlighting the ineffectiveness of bans—a narrative commonly aligned with left-leaning critiques; (3) Explanatory prose centers on mechanisms circumventing bans (interstate travel, mail-order pills under shield laws) in neutral-to-validating terms, with no countervailing legal, enforcement, or policy perspectives; (4) Declines are downplayed (Texas decrease labeled “small,” Oklahoma decline discounted by noting an “unusually large” 2020 baseline), reinforcing the overarching narrative of increases; (5) Only one expert voice is featured and no opposing or balancing viewpoints are included, reflecting one-sided sourcing in a politically charged topic.
